title: Gambas Pil Pil
subtitle: Spanish sizzling garlic prawns
excerpt: Prawns cooked in a pool of olive oil with sliced garlic and chilli, served at the table still hissing in a hot cazuela. The best five-minute tapa there is.
sourceNotes: Gambas al pil pil (or gambas al ajillo) is one of the most canonical Spanish tapas, served throughout Andalusia and the rest of Spain. The pil pil name refers to the sizzling sound of the oil. Documented in Penelope Casas, Tapas: The Little Dishes of Spain (1985).

content.text: Gambas pil pil is technically simpler than almost any tapa (garlic, oil, chilli, prawns) but requires attention. The garlic must go in before the prawns, in warm rather than hot oil, so it softens and flavours the oil without burning. Then the heat increases, the prawns go in, and you have about 2 minutes before they overcook.

Raw shell-on prawns give the best result; the shells flavour the oil as they cook. Peeled work fine if that is what you have. Either way, watch the colour: as soon as the prawns turn entirely pink and opaque, they are done. Remove from the heat immediately and serve in the same pan.

items.prepNote: shell-on if possible; peeled works too

content.text: Put the olive oil and garlic into a cold frying pan. Place over medium heat and cook, stirring occasionally, until the garlic is just beginning to turn pale gold — about 3 minutes. Add the chilli flakes and stir for 30 seconds. Increase the heat to high, add the prawns, and cook, tossing occasionally, until pink and curled, about 2 minutes. Season with salt and a squeeze of lemon. Scatter with parsley if using. Serve immediately in the pan with bread to mop the oil.

items.cause: Oil too hot when the garlic went in
items.fix: Start in cold oil — the garlic heats gradually as the oil warms; this gives control over the colour

items.cause: Overcooked — prawns need only 2 minutes total
items.fix: Remove from heat the moment all the prawns are pink and opaque; residual heat continues to cook them briefly

content.text: Gambas pil pil are found in every tapas bar in Spain, traditionally served in a small terracotta cazuela that arrives at the table still sizzling. The name comes from the Basque word for the crackling of oil, giving the dish one of the more evocative names in Spanish cooking. Bread is not an accompaniment; it is obligatory.
